2094A - Trippi Troppi

Problem Credits: Proof_by_QED

Solution

This can be solved by printing the zero-th index of each string in sequence. For example, suppose your strings are aa, bb, and cc. Then in C++, you can use cout << a[0] << b[0] << c[0] << '\n';, and similarly, in Python you can use print(a[0] + b[0] + c[0]). See your preferred language's syntax for how to obtain a given indexed character from a string.

Code
t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    inp = input()
    for w in inp.split():
        print(w[0],end="")
    print()
Rate The Problem!
  • 271 Good Problem
  • 175 Okay Problem
  • 26 Bad Problem
  • 16 Didn't Solve

2094B - Bobritto Bandito

Problem Credits: Proof_by_QED

Hint

What is the condition for l′l′ and r′r′ to be valid?

Answer

We must have r′−l′=mr′−l′=m and l≤l′≤0≤r′≤rl≤l′≤0≤r′≤r.

Solution

The problem reduces to finding l′l′ and r′r′ such that r′−l′=mr′−l′=m and l≤l′≤0≤r′≤rl≤l′≤0≤r′≤r. There are several different approaches; two are outlined below.

One approach which runs in O(1)O(1) time is to case on whether m≤rm≤r. If m≤rm≤r, we can choose l′=0l′=0 and r′=mr′=m, and then we see that r′−l′=m−0=mr′−l′=m−0=m and l≤0≤0≤m≤rl≤0≤0≤m≤r as desired. If m>rm>r, we can choose l′=r−ml′=r−m and r′=rr′=r, and then we see that r′−l′=r−(r−m)=mr′−l′=r−(r−m)=m and l≤r−m≤0≤r≤rl≤r−m≤0≤r≤r, where l≤r−ml≤r−m holds because r−l=n≥mr−l=n≥m.

An alternative approach which runs in O(m)O(m) time is to simply simulate the process; expand in an arbitrary direction which satisfies the desired inequalities until r′−l′=mr′−l′=m.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define int long long

void solve() {
    int n, m, l, r; cin >> n >> m >> l >> r;
    int diff = n - m;
    l = abs(l);
    if (l >= diff) {
        l -= diff;
        diff = 0;
    }
    else {
        diff -= l;
        l = 0;
    }
    cout << -l << " " << r - diff << '\n';
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t; cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
    return 0;
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 288 Good Problem
  • 101 Okay Problem
  • 99 Bad Problem
  • 41 Didn't Solve

2094C - Brr Brrr Patapim

Problem Credits: cry

Hint

How can we find pipi for 2≤i≤n2≤i≤n? How can we find pipi for n+1≤i≤2nn+1≤i≤2n?

Answer

For 2≤i≤n2≤i≤n, we can find pipi by checking G1,i−1G1,i−1. For n+1≤i≤2nn+1≤i≤2n, we can find pipi by checking Gn,i−nGn,i−n.

Solution

Observe that for all 2≤i≤n2≤i≤n, we can find pipi by checking G1,i−1G1,i−1. Then for all n+1≤i≤2nn+1≤i≤2n, we can find pipi by checking Gn,i−nGn,i−n. Now, we only have to find the value of p1p1. However, each value from 11 to 2n2n must appear exactly once in pp. Thus, we can simply find the value which has not appeared yet, and choose that as p1p1. This can be done by storing which values have been seen in a boolean array, and then finding which one remains false.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define int long long

void solve() {
    int n; cin >> n;
    vector<int> ans(2*n+1, 0);
    vector<bool> used(2*n+1, false);
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
        for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {
            int x; cin >> x;
            ans[i + j] = x;
            used[x] = true;
        }
    }
    for (int i = 1; i <= 2 * n; i++) {
        if (ans[i] != 0) cout << ans[i] << " ";
        else {
            for (int j = 1; j <= n * 2; j++) {
                if (!used[j]) {
                    used[j] = true;
                    cout << j << " ";
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    cout << "\n";
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t; cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
    return 0;
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 291 Good Problem
  • 61 Okay Problem
  • 34 Bad Problem
  • 16 Didn't Solve

2094D - Tung Tung Sahur

Problem Credits: Proof_by_QED

Hint

What would happen if s1s1 consists of only LL?

Answer

In this case, the answer is yes if and only if |s1|≤|s2|≤2|s1||s1|≤|s2|≤2|s1|. Now try to generalize this.

Solution

First, let us consider a simpler case of the problem: suppose that there is only LL in both s1s1 and s2s2. Then it is clear that the answer is yes if and only if |s1|≤|s2|≤2|s1||s1|≤|s2|≤2|s1|. If this holds (in particular, we also necessitate that s1s1 and s2s2 consist of only a single character, and the same character), we will call s2s2 an extension of s1s1.

Now, observe that the problem given is simply the simpler version, repeated several times alternating between LL and RR. So we can partition s1s1 into "blocks" (where we define a block to be a maximal group of contiguous identical characters). Then the answer is yes if and only if s2s2 is the concatenation of extensions of blocks in s1s1. For example, consider s1=LLLLLRLs1=LLLLLRL. We see that this consists of five LLs, one RR, and one LL. So the answer is yes if and only if s2s2 consists of between five and ten LLs (inclusive), between one and two RRs, and between one and two LL 's, concatenated in that order.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define int long long

void solve() {
    string a, b; cin >> a >> b;
    int n = a.size();
    int m = b.size();
    if (m < n || m > 2 * n || a[0] != b[0]) {
        cout << "NO\n";
        return;
    }
    vector<int> aa, bb;
    int cnt = 1;
    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        if (a[i] != a[i-1]) {
            aa.push_back(cnt);
            cnt = 1;
        }
        else cnt++;
    }
    aa.push_back(cnt);
    cnt = 1;
    for (int i = 1; i < m; i++) {
        if (b[i] != b[i-1]) {
            bb.push_back(cnt);
            cnt = 1;
        }
        else cnt++;
    }
    bb.push_back(cnt);
    if (aa.size() != bb.size()) {
        cout << "NO\n";
        return;
    }
    n = aa.size();
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        if (aa[i] > bb[i] || aa[i] * 2 < bb[i]) {
            cout << "NO\n";
            return;
        }
    }
    cout << "YES\n";
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t; cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
    return 0;
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 496 Good Problem
  • 63 Okay Problem
  • 39 Bad Problem
  • 69 Didn't Solve

2094E - Boneca Ambalabu

Problem Credits: Proof_by_QED

Hint 1

Consider each bit independently.

Hint 2

Suppose we fix kk. How can we compute the desired sum quickly? Note that this may require preprocessing.

Solution

Here, it suffices to consider each bit independently, because addition is both commutative and associative. For 0≤i<300≤i<30, let cnticnti denote the number of elements of aa which have the ii-th bit set (where bits are indexed from the least significant bit being the 00-th bit); note that these can be found in O(30n)O(30n) time (more generally, we need O(logmaxai)O(log⁡maxai) operations per element).

Now, suppose we choose a particular kk. Then we can find the sum (ak⊕a1)+(ak⊕a2)⋯+(ak⊕an)(ak⊕a1)+(ak⊕a2)⋯+(ak⊕an) as follows: for a given bit position ii, the number of elements ajaj such that ak⊕ajak⊕aj has the ii-th bit set will be cnticnti if the ii-th bit of akak is not set, and n−cntin−cnti if the ii-th bit of akak is set. So we can simply add cnti⋅2icnti⋅2i to our sum if the ii-th bit of akak is not set, and add (n−cnti)⋅2i(n−cnti)⋅2i to our sum if the ii-th bit of akak is set.

This allows us to compute (ak⊕a1)+(ak⊕a2)⋯+(ak⊕an)(ak⊕a1)+(ak⊕a2)⋯+(ak⊕an) for any kk in O(30)O(30) time, so we can now compute this sum for all kk in O(30n)O(30n) time and output the maximum.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define int long long

void solve() {
    int n; cin >> n;
    int arr[n+1];
    vector<int> cnt(30, 0);
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
        cin >> arr[i];
        for (int j = 0; j < 30; j++) {
            cnt[j] += ((arr[i] >> j) & 1);
        }
    }
    int ans = 0;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
        int tot = 0;
        for (int j = 0; j < 30; j++) {
            bool f = ((arr[i] >> j) & 1);
            if (f) tot += (1 << j) * (n - cnt[j]);
            else tot += (1 << j) * cnt[j];
        }
        ans = max(ans, tot);
    }
    cout << ans << "\n";
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t; cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
    return 0;
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 656 Good Problem
  • 30 Okay Problem
  • 14 Bad Problem
  • 128 Didn't Solve

2094F - Trulimero Trulicina

Problem Credits: Proof_by_QED

Hint

What would happen if we tried outputting the numbers 1,…,k,1,…,k,…1,…,k,1,…,k,… in the natural reading order? For example, for n=3n=3, m=4m=4, and k=6k=6, we would output the following:

1 2 3 4
5 6 1 2
3 4 5 6

In which cases does this fail?

Answer

This fails if and only if mm is a multiple of kk, because we see that horizontally adjacent elements differ by 1(modk)1(modk) and vertically adjacent elements differ by m(modk)m(modk). Now try to resolve this case.

Solution

There are many working constructions for this problem; one of them is to case on whether or not mm is a multiple of kk.

Suppose mm is not a multiple of kk. For example, consider the second sample, where n=3n=3, m=4m=4, and k=6k=6. Then we can output the numbers 1,…,k,1,…,k,…1,…,k,1,…,k,… in the natural reading order, as follows:

1 2 3 4
5 6 1 2
3 4 5 6

and we see that any two horizontally adjacent elements differ by 1(modk)1(modk), whereas any two vertically adjacent elements differ by m(modk)m(modk), so no two adjacent elements are the same, as desired.

Now, suppose mm is a multiple of kk. For example, consider the case n=4n=4, m=6m=6, and k=3k=3. Suppose we were to try the above strategy, then we get:

1 2 3 1 2 3
1 2 3 1 2 3
1 2 3 1 2 3
1 2 3 1 2 3

which doesn't work, since vertically adjacent elements are the same. We can fix this by cyclically shifting every other row as follows:

1 2 3 1 2 3
2 3 1 2 3 1
1 2 3 1 2 3
2 3 1 2 3 1

and we see that any two horizontally adjacent elements differ by 1(modk)1(modk) as before, whereas any two vertically adjacent elements also differ by 1(modk)1(modk), so no two adjacent elements are the same, as desired.

Code
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline
for _ in range(int(input())):
    n,m,k = map(int,input().split())
    LAST = [-1 for _ in range(m)]
    for i in range(n):
        shift = False
        CUR = [0 for _ in range(m)]
        for j in range(m):
            elm = ((i * m + j) % k) + 1
            if elm == LAST[j]:
                shift = True
            CUR[j] = elm
        if shift:
            CUR = [CUR[(j+1)%m] for j in range(m)]
        print(*CUR)
        LAST = CUR
Rate The Problem!
  • 340 Good Problem
  • 74 Okay Problem
  • 140 Bad Problem
  • 64 Didn't Solve

2094G - Chimpanzini Bananini

Problem Credits: Proof_by_QED, cry

Hint 1

Note that reversing an array then pushing an element to the back is similar to simply pushing an element to the front. Thus, we would like to push elements to both ends of the array. What data structure supports this?

Answer

A deque is the most natural choice to use. Most languages, including C++, Java, and Python, include deques in their standard library, so you likely do not have to implement it yourself.

Hint 2

What other values do we need to maintain in order to keep track of score?

Answer

It suffices to keep track of the current score, the score of the array if it were backwards, the size of the array, and the sum of the array.

Solution

We can solve this using a deque. Let scorescore denote the current score, and rscorerscore denote the score of the array if it were backwards. Let sizesize denote the size of the array, and sumsum denote the sum of the array. Then we consider how these three values change as each operation is performed.

Suppose operation 1 is performed. This is equivalent to popping the back element of the array and pushing it in the front. When you pop the back element of the array, scorescore decreases by an⋅sizean⋅size. Then when you push it to the front, scorescore increases by sumsum because anan is pushed to the first spot and every element from a1a1 to an−1an−1 moves forward one spot. Notice that rscorerscore changes in the reverse way; this is equivalent to popping the front element of the array and pushing it to the back. When you pop the front element of the array, rscorerscore decreases by sumsum, and when you push it to the back, rscorerscore increases by an⋅sizean⋅size. Note that sizesize and sumsum remain unchanged.

Suppose operation 2 is performed. Then we swap scorescore and rscorerscore, and we also want to "reverse" the array. However, it is costly to entirely reverse the deque. Instead, we will set a flag to indicate that the array has been reversed (and similarly, if the flag is already set, we will unset it). If the flag is set, we simply switch the front and back ends whenever we access or modify the deque while performing operations 1 or 3.

Suppose operation 3 is performed. Then we see that sizesize increases by 11 and sumsum increases by kk. Then scorescore increases by k⋅sizek⋅size and rscorerscore increases by sumsum (using the new value of sumsum), by identical reasoning to that in operation 1.

Additional optimization: we don't actually have to maintain rscorerscore; we can obtain it with the expression: (n+1)∑ni=1ai−score(n+1)∑i=1nai−score. This is because score+rscore=(n+1)∑ni=1aiscore+rscore=(n+1)∑i=1nai since the ii-th term is counted ii times in scorescore and n+1−in+1−i times in rscorerscore.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define int long long

void solve() {
    int norm = 0, rev = 0;
    int q; cin >> q;
    int tot = 0;
    int n = 0;
    deque<int> qNorm, qRev;
    int p = 0;
    while (q--) {
        int s; cin >> s;
        if (s == 1) {
            int last = qNorm.back();
            qNorm.pop_back();
            qNorm.push_front(last);
            norm += (tot - last);
            norm -= last * n;
            norm += last;

            last = qRev.front();
            qRev.pop_front();
            qRev.push_back(last);
            rev -= (tot - last);
            rev += last * n;
            rev -= last;
        }
        else if (s == 2) {
            swap(rev, norm);
            swap(qNorm, qRev);
        }
        else if (s == 3) {
            n++;
            int k; cin >> k;
            qNorm.push_back(k);
            qRev.push_front(k);
            norm += k * n;
            rev += tot;
            rev += k;
            tot += k;
        }
        cout << norm << "\n";
    }
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t; cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
    return 0;
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 414 Good Problem
  • 38 Okay Problem
  • 10 Bad Problem
  • 50 Didn't Solve

2094H - La Vaca Saturno Saturnita

Problem Credits: cry

Hint 1

What must the value of a[i]a[i] be in order for kk to change?

Answer

a[i]a[i] must be a divisor of kk.

Hint 2

Can we use this to bound the number of times kk changes?

Answer

For each divisor dd of kk, kk can only change once, because after the first iteration with a[i]=da[i]=d, kk is no longer divisible by dd. Thus, the number of times kk changes is bounded by the number of divisors of kk.

Solution

Consider a particular query. We make the following two observations: first, the value of kk only changes if a[i]a[i] is a divisor of kk, and second, if there exists a[i1]=a[i2]a[i1]=a[i2] with l≤i1<i2≤rl≤i1<i2≤r, then the value of kk will not change at i=i2i=i2 (because after the iteration i=i1i=i1, we have that kk is no longer divisible by a[i2]a[i2]).

This allows us to bound the number of times kk changes by d(k)d(k), where d(k)d(k) is the number of divisors of kk. Note that the divisors of d(k)d(k) can be found in O(k−−√)O(k) time by checking if aa divides kk for every aa from 22 to k−−√k, and if so, aa and kaka are both divisors of kk. Furthermore, at the scale constrained by the problem bounds, d(k)d(k) is around O(k−−√3)O(k3), so we only have to update kk at most O(k−−√3)O(k3) times.

Now, we would like to find the first time each divisor of kk appears in a[l],…,a[r]a[l],…,a[r]. This can be done by storing the array aa in a map, where each value is mapped to a vector of indices where that values appears. Then for a given divisor of kk, we can use lower_bound() (or generally, binary search) to find the smallest index at or after ll where this divisor appears, and we can check if it is no greater than rr.

Now, we have a list of indices at which kk might change. So, suppose that kk changes to k′k′ at index i1i1, and then changes to k′′k″ at index i2i2 (and these are adjacent changes). Then we know that we have a value of k′k′ from i=i1i=i1 to i2−1i2−1, so we can add k′⋅(i2−i1)k′⋅(i2−i1) to ansans. We can then do this for all changes. This allows us to solve the problem in O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) preprocessing time and O(k−−√+d(k)logn)O(k+d(k)log⁡n) time per query.

There are a few ways to optimize the runtime further, if your implementation is too slow. We can use a vector (of vectors) instead of a map, since A=maxai=105A=maxai=105 is reasonably small. Note that instantiating a vector of this size in every test case is too slow, so you may have to instantiate it globally and clear the vectors that were used after each test case.

Another optimization is to preprocess divisors instead of computing them on the spot. We can compute and store the divisors of all integers 2≤ai≤1052≤ai≤105 in O(AlogA)O(Alog⁡A) time in a vector of vectors where divisor[ai]divisor[ai] contains all divisors of aiai as follows: for all 2≤i≤A2≤i≤A, we push ii into divisors[j]divisors[j] for all multiples jj of ii. (The runtime follows from the fact that there are ⌊Ai⌋⌊Ai⌋ multiples of ii which are at most AA, so across all ii, we have at most A1+A2+⋯+AA=A(11+⋯+1A)=AH(A)=O(AlogA)A1+A2+⋯+AA=A(11+⋯+1A)=AH(A)=O(Alog⁡A) computations). This allows us to remove the k−−√k term in the runtime of each query.

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
#define int long long

void solve() {
    map<int, vector<int>> pos;
    map<int, int> ptr;
    int n, q; cin >> n >> q;
    int arr[n+1];
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
        cin >> arr[i];
        pos[arr[i]].push_back(i);
        ptr[arr[i]] = 0;
    }
    vector<tuple<int, int, int, int>> qr(q);
    int c = 0;
    for (auto &[l, r, v, i]: qr) {
        cin >> v >> l >> r;
        i = c++;
    }
    vector<int> anss(q);
    sort(qr.begin(), qr.end());
    int prevL = 1;
    for (auto [l, r, v, idd]: qr) {
        for (int j = prevL; j < l; j++) {
            ptr[arr[j]]++;
        }
        prevL = l;
        vector<pair<int, int>> facts;
        for (int i = 1; i * i <= v; i++) {
            if (v % i == 0) {
                int fact1 = v / i;
                if (!(pos[fact1].size() == 0 || ptr[fact1] >= pos[fact1].size() || pos[fact1][ptr[fact1]] > r || fact1 == 1)) {
                    facts.push_back({pos[fact1][ptr[fact1]], fact1});
                }
                int fact2 = i;
                if (fact2 == fact1) continue;
                if (!(pos[fact2].size() == 0 || ptr[fact2] >= pos[fact2].size() || pos[fact2][ptr[fact2]] > r || fact2 == 1)) {
                    facts.push_back({pos[fact2][ptr[fact2]], fact2});
                }
            }
        }
        sort(facts.begin(), facts.end());
        int pr = l;
        int ans = 0;
        for (auto [idx, val]: facts) {
            int rr = idx - pr;
            ans += v * rr;
            while (v % val == 0) v /= val;
            pr = idx;
        }
        if (facts.empty()) ans = v * (r - l + 1);
        else ans += (r - pr + 1) * v;
        anss[idd] = ans;
    }
    for (int i = 0; i < q; i++) cout << anss[i] << "\n";
}

signed main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.tie(0);
    int t; cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
    return 0;
}
Rate The Problem!
  • 236 Good Problem
  • 35 Okay Problem
  • 14 Bad Problem
  • 116 Didn't Solve
Tutorial of Codeforces Round 1017 (Div. 4)
  • +90

  • 12 months ago

I hope you all enjoyed the contest!

This is my first time writing an editorial — please let me know if there's anything unclear (or any mistakes). I'll read all of the replies to this comment and make any edits as necessary.

P.S. Thanks to temporary1 for proofreading, and for suggesting the optimizations on H.

    paulk93

    paulk93
    12 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    For hint 2 for problem G:

    "It suffices to keep track of the current score, the score of the array if it were backwards, the size of the array, and the sum of the array."

    No. You also need to know the last element.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      reirugan
      12 months ago, show (+4) #
      »
      »
      »
      reirugan
      12 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      +3

      In Hint 1, it says that we will be using a deque. In Hint 2, I ask what other values we have to maintain. These are the only other values we need to maintain; the last element is already being tracked by the deque.

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        paulk93
        12 months ago, show (+3) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        paulk93
        12 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        What does "other" mean when you say "other values"? If you don't count the 'last element', why do you count 'the size'; isn't that also 'being tracked by the deque'?

        → Reply
        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          reirugan
          12 months ago, show (+2) #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          reirugan
          12 months ago, hide # ^ |
           
          +1

          You're right that in most languages, the standard library deque supports constant-time access of size, so it doesn't really have to be maintained. But technically a deque is just a data structure which supports pushing/popping and accessing on both ends — it doesn't by definition necessarily support size.

          In any case, does a small wording difference like this really matter so much?

          → Reply
          • »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            paulk93
            12 months ago, show (+1) #
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            paulk93
            12 months ago, hide # ^ |
             
            0

            I don't view it as wording (otherwise I wouldn't be commenting about it). The size and sum of the array are very trivial to maintain. The problem, to me, was all about how to maintain the last element. Using two deques is clever and smarter than what I did.

            → Reply
            • »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              reirugan
              12 months ago, show #
              »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              »
              reirugan
              12 months ago, hide # ^ |
              ← Rev. 2 →  
              0

              You shouldn't need two deques, only one. (You can use two deques if you want, but the solution I presented only uses one.)

              The idea of using a deque is in Hint 1, so I think semantically it's not necessary to include in Hint 2.

              → Reply

In F I tried to fill the board in chess board manner, like filling all the black squares first and then all the white squares, what is the problem with this approach?

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Krrishchanchal
    12 months ago, show (+8) #
    »
    »
    Krrishchanchal
    12 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    maybe number of elements are not same. share exacxt approach?

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      amey75
      12 months ago, show (+6) #
      »
      »
      »
      amey75
      12 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0 
      // this is the code
int main()
{
    fast;
    ll t;
    cin>>t;
    while(t--)
    {
        ll n, m, lmao;
        cin>>n>>m>>lmao;

        ll count = (n*m)/lmao;

        vector<pair<ll, ll>> numbers;
        for(int i = 1;i<=lmao;i++) numbers.push_back({i, count});

        vector<vector<ll>> grid(n, vector<ll>(m ,0));

        int k = 0;
        for(int i = 0;i<n;i++)
        {
            int start = 1;
            if((i%2) == 0) start = 0;

            for(int j = start;j<m;j+=2)
            {
                grid[i][j] = numbers[k].first;
                numbers[k].second--;
                if(numbers[k].second == 0) k++;
            }
        }

        for(int i = 0;i<n;i++)
        {
            int start = 0;
            if((i%2) == 0) start = 1;

            for(int j = start;j<m;j+=2)
            {
                grid[i][j] = numbers[k].first;
                numbers[k].second--;
                if(numbers[k].second == 0) k++;
            }
        }

        for(int i = 0;i<n;i++)
        {
            for(int j = 0;j<m;j++)
            {
                cout<<grid[i][j]<<" ";
            }
            cout<<endl;
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

        I had a very similar implementation. I saw that it didn't work. In this case row is n and col is m: I said: if (n < m) { swap(n, m); switched = true; }

        then at the end I dealt with printing the normal ones and the switched ones as two separate cases so I can get them back in format. It worked.. I don't know why this WOULD work, though. My submission (PASCALABC.NET): 320834015

Why is the time for query in H O(sqrt(k)+d(k)logn)? Shouldn't it be more since we have to go for every divisor not only for k, but for k',k'' and so on?

    Miteusz

    »

    12 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Every divisor or k',k'' and so on is also a divisor of k.

    → Reply
»
nika-skybytska
12 months ago, show #
»
nika-skybytska
12 months ago, hide # |
 
+11

I have yet another optimization for H: 315473086. Instead of binary searching, you can store a queue of positions greater than or equal to the current. Then, you can access its first element in constant time. This requires you to process queries offline and in parallel: perform one jump for all queries starting at the current position before removing it from the queue.

→ Reply
»

    • »

      »
      »
      aniketmangal
      12 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Ohh got it, Thankyou!

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        kr25161
        12 months ago, show #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        kr25161
        12 months ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 6 →  
        +3

        regardless of the parity of n and m, for this strategy to work we want nm/3 < (m(n-1)/2) + 1 because for k = 2 it always pass if nm/3 < rhs nm/4 also follows

        nm/3 < m(n-1)/2 + 1

        nm/3 <= m(n-1)/2

        n/3 <= (n-1)/2

        2n <= 3n — 3

        n >= 3

        solution only works for n >= 3. what is (m(n-1)/2) + 1 if your doubt, just start coloring some square from bottom go up and count the no of cells the first time they touch. like this

        → Reply
»

This is the first time I fail a problem because of declaring a vector locally instead of globally [H]. After getting the first TLE, I just assumed that what I was doing wasn't efficient enough and never thought to check for something as stupid as that... I guess for future reference, think more about what should be global and what shouldn't? But still, I can't believe I actually missed that.

  • »

    12 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    neelm1002
    12 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Not 100% sure but i think it is due to while loop being slower than fixed for loop at cpu/compiler level as the operations are seen as more friendly/efficient to run. Also maybe more branching or frequent shifts in the first code. All of these are low level differences but they might have accumulated to push over time limit to cause TLE. ALso generally submit using PyPy as it is quicker than submitting with Python. You dont have to edit any part of your code for it.

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    -firefly-
    12 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    -firefly-
    12 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    During a naive Python explanation, it does take more time to access a global variable than a local variable.

    Spoiler

    If you choose Python 3.13.2 as your language in SUBMIT tab, a tip saying " Almost always, if you send a solution on PyPy, it works much faster" will show. Therefore, DO NOT use Python 3.13.2 at any time, and use PyPy 3.10 (7.3.15, 64bit) instead.

yoshi_likes_e5
12 months ago, show #
»
yoshi_likes_e5
12 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 3 →  
0

If you prune the divisors after each iteration, and combined with offline processing, you can get O(d)O(d) for each query.

Proof

Let A[u]A[u] being the exponent of uu in the prime factorization of AiAi, and K[u]K[u] being the exponent of uu in the prime factorization of KK.

The operation described in the problem is: Take min(⌊K[u]A[u]⌋)∀A[u]>0min(⌊K[u]A[u]⌋)∀A[u]>0, let it be xx. Then, for all prime uu, K[u]:=K[u]−x∗A[u]K[u]:=K[u]−x∗A[u].

We consider the case when x>0x>0. Note that the number of divisors is ∏(K[u]+1)∏(K[u]+1) [1].

Consider u=argmin(⌊K[u]A[u]⌋∀A[u]>0,⌊K[u]A[u]⌋>0)u=argmin(⌊K[u]A[u]⌋∀A[u]>0,⌊K[u]A[u]⌋>0).

Clearly, the operation described above is K[u]:=K[u]modA[u]K[u]:=K[u]modA[u] for this uu.

We have to prove that K[u]modA[u]+1K[u]+1≤12∀A[u]≤K[u]K[u]modA[u]+1K[u]+1≤12∀A[u]≤K[u] [2].

This is equivalent to K[u]>2∗(K[u]modA[u])∀A[u]≤K[u]K[u]>2∗(K[u]modA[u])∀A[u]≤K[u].

Proof by contradiction: Assume K[u]≤2∗(K[u]modA[u])K[u]≤2∗(K[u]modA[u]).

Then, K[u]<2∗A[u]K[u]<2∗A[u] since K[u]modA[u]<A[u]K[u]modA[u]<A[u].

Since A[u]≤K[u]<2∗A[u]A[u]≤K[u]<2∗A[u], we have K[u]modA[u]=K[u]−A[u]K[u]modA[u]=K[u]−A[u].

Then, K[u]≤2∗(K[u]−A[u])K[u]≤2∗(K[u]−A[u]) or K[u]−2∗A[u]≥0K[u]−2∗A[u]≥0, a contradiction.

From [1] and [2], we conclude that after each index that modifies kk, the number of divisors of kk decrease at least by half. Using the geometric series, we conclude that the sum of number of divisors of kk after every iteration is bounded by 2∗d2∗d (QED).

→ Reply
»

H_a_r_M_i_t
12 months ago, hide # |
 
0

I used following approach for 2094F - Trulimero Trulicina

We aim to construct an n×mn×m grid using integers from 11 to kk, each appearing exactly n⋅mkn⋅mk times, with no two adjacent cells having the same value.

When one of the dimensions is divisible by kk:

  • Case A: (nmodk=0)(nmodk=0)

    • Fill the grid column-wise using: a[i][j] = ((i + j) % k) + 1
    • The offset changes per column to ensure adjacent cells differ.

  • Case B: (mmodk=0)(mmodk=0)

    • Fill the grid row-wise using: a[i][j] = ((j + i) % k) + 1

These cyclic patterns rotate values so that no adjacent cells share the same value.

When neither nn nor mm is divisible by kk:

  • Factor kk into two integers nnnn and mmmm such that:

    • nn⋅mm=knn⋅mm=k
    • nn∣nnn∣n and mm∣mmm∣m
    • nn≥2nn≥2, mm≥2mm≥2 This ensures the tile is at least (2×2)(2×2), avoiding adjacency conflicts.

  • Steps:

    • Build a tile of size nn×mmnn×mm filled with values from 11 to kk.
    • Replicate the tile across the grid using: a[i][j] = tile[i % nn][j % mm]

This guarantees: Equal frequency and no two adjacent cells share the same value.

Summary

  • Use cyclic shifts when nn or mm is divisible by kk.
  • Use (≥2)(≥2) ×× (≥2)(≥2) tiling when neither is divisible.

Check 315555434 for implementation if you wish to :D

→ Reply

Can someone please tell me why my solution to H giving TLE : 315611720

instead this slight change in the above solution passes : 315624522

Only difference is that now i am clearing only the filled 'pos' array of vector, previously i am clearing all the 'pos' array of vector upto 'n',

but my doubt is, it is given that sum of 'n' over all the test_case is '1e5', so if i run clear over all upto '1e5' (assuming each 'i' can have max 1e2 factors) then to clear upto 'n', we need T.C. of O(1e5*1e2 == 1e7) which will be easily runnable, am i correct?

upd : got it, as it will only del upto n index but what about element greater than n, those will added continuously leads to memory overflow and TLE

ATSTNG
12 months ago, show #
»
ATSTNG
12 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 3 →  
0

I have a solution for 2094H - La Vaca Saturno Saturnita with a better bound.

Precompute divisors of all numbers in O(AlogA)O(Alog⁡A).

Each query will be processed in this way:

  1. Iterate all divisors of current kk and find the closest element to the right that is a divisor of kk
  2. Jump to that element and add k⋅(i2−i1)k⋅(i2−i1) to answer
  3. Apply described loop operation naively on the dividing element reducing kk to k′k′
  4. Add k′k′ to answer and advance by 11 position
  5. Repeat until we have reached rr

Process all queries in parallel, so that information about closest element with given value is available in O(1)O(1) for all values at any point.

This operation 

while k is divisible by a[i]:
    k := k/a[i]

has 22 limitations:

(1) It reduces the value of kk by at least twice on each iteration. It applies the limit on total number of loop iterations as O(logk)O(log⁡k). This is also the limit on number of jumps.

(2) It reduces the number of divisors of kk or by at least twice after all iterations on a single element a[i]a[i]. Consider factorizations of kk and a[i]a[i]

F(k,p)=(1+F(k/p,p))ifkmodp=0else0F(k,p)=(1+F(k/p,p))ifkmodp=0else0

k=∏p∈Ppow(p,F(k,p))k=∏p∈Ppow(p,F(k,p))

d(k)=∏p∈P1+F(k,p)d(k)=∏p∈P1+F(k,p)

For any kk and a[i]a[i] that kmoda[i]=0kmoda[i]=0 there exists such prime pp that

F(k′,p)=F(k,p)%F(a[i],p)F(k′,p)=F(k,p)%F(a[i],p)

then

2⋅F(k′,p)<F(k,p)2⋅F(k′,p)<F(k,p)

2⋅(F(k′,p)+1)⩽F(k,p)+12⋅(F(k′,p)+1)⩽F(k,p)+1

2⋅d(k′)⩽d(k)2⋅d(k′)⩽d(k)

Thus instead of logklog⁡k jumps O(d(k))O(d(k)) each, we actually have d(k)+d(k′)+d(k′′)+…=d(k)+d(k)/2+d(k)/4+…=∑i=0log2kd(k)2i<2⋅d(k)d(k)+d(k′)+d(k″)+…=d(k)+d(k)/2+d(k)/4+…=∑i=0log2kd(k)2i<2⋅d(k)

Compexity per query is then O(logk+d(k))O(log⁡k+d(k)). Total complexity is then O(AlogA+qlogk+q⋅d(k))O(Alog⁡A+qlog⁡k+q⋅d(k))

315489102

→ Reply
»

Shouldn't it be 0 if the bit is 1? since 1 xor 1 will be 0.

Shouldn't it be cnt[j] if the bit is 0? since 0 xor 1 will be 1.

← Rev. 2 →  
0

For 2094C — Brr Brrr Patapim ----- More optimised Code than the editorial one

void solve(){
    int n;
    cin>>n;
    vector<vector<int>>mat(n,vector<int>(n));
    for(int i=0;i<n;i++){
        for(int j=0;j<n;j++){
            cin>>mat[i][j];
        }
    }

    // main
    
    vector<int>ans(2*n);
    int actualSum=2*n*(2*n+1)/2;
    int sum=0,k=1;

    for(int j=0;j<n;j++){
        ans[k++]=mat[0][j];
        sum += mat[0][j];
    } 
    for(int i=1;i<n;i++){
        ans[k++]=mat[i][n-1];
        sum += mat[i][n-1];
    }
    ans[0] = actualSum - sum;

    for(int i=0;i<2*n;i++) cout<<ans[i]<<" ";
    cout<<"\n";
}

Using the logic of missing number to find the p1
